title: Actor Ben McKenzie Explores Cryptocurrency Fraud in Doc.

contentText: Actor Ben McKenzie, known for his role in The OC, discusses his transition from acting to investigating cryptocurrency through a new film he wrote and directed: Everyone Is Lying to You for Money. McKenzie explains to Bloomberg This Weekend's David Gura and Christina Ruffini he was motivated by pandemic boredom and a suggestion to buy Bitcoin. (Source: Bloomberg)

title: Retailer selling broken RTX 5090 GPUs for as low as $1,760 — GPUs were damaged during transport, but include all components on the PCB
snippet: A French retailer is willing to sell you defective RTX 5090s for half of what they cost new, on the condition that you'll be able to repair or recycle them. You can either get a random 5090 variant, depending on stock,…
contentText: A French retailer is willing to sell you defective RTX 5090s for half of what they cost new, on the condition that you'll be able to repair or recycle them. You can either get a random 5090 variant, depending on stock, for around $1,760 or pay almost $2,000 specifically for an MSI Ventus 3X OC model. The units come with transport-related damage.

contentText: The FCC estimates that roughly 75% of all U.S.-bound electronics are currently tested in Chinese facilities.

title: Vibe coding is spreading, but engineering is not going away
snippet: Generative AI is making it easier for non-programmers to build small tools, but the harder work still starts after the first version runs.
contentText: Generative AI has turned coding from a specialized skill into something anyone can do with a simple prompt. A warehouse owner is revolutionizing his shipping software with AI and a creative designer vibe coded her first app with zero technical experience. Computer programmers are doing it too, but a director at Google Cloud AI says vibe coding does not mean the end of serious engineering. As professionals and hobbyists alike are writing code with AI, hiring for junior developers is falling fast and researchers warn companies are making a dangerous short-term bet. (Source: Bloomberg)
summary: Bloomberg Technology looks at how generative AI is letting non-programmers build simple software. The bigger question is who reviews, secures, and maintains that code once it enters a real business.
articleText:
Bloomberg Technology’s latest segment captures a real shift: people who never thought of themselves as programmers are now using generative AI to build small pieces of software. A warehouse owner can automate shipping work. A designer can try building an app. Professional developers are using the same tools to move faster.

That does not make software engineering disappear. It changes where the hard work sits. The first draft of an app may arrive faster, but someone still has to understand the system, test the edge cases, secure the data, and decide whether the code is safe enough to run inside a business.

The risk for companies is treating a working demo as a finished product. If teams skip review because the prototype feels easy, they can trade short-term speed for long-term maintenance debt. That matters as junior developer hiring slows and more organizations lean on AI tools before they have clear rules for ownership.

For AI infrastructure companies, vibe coding is also a demand signal. More people building with AI means more pressure on developer platforms, managed guardrails, cloud services, and compute. The winners will not be the teams that generate the most code. They will be the teams that turn AI-assisted coding into reliable software.

Watch whether enterprises can measure productivity gains after the novelty fades, and whether they pair faster code generation with stronger review, testing, and security practices.
expertLensShort: Vibe coding lowers the barrier to building software, but it raises the value of engineering judgment, code review, and operational ownership.

expertLensFull.thesis: Vibe coding lowers the barrier to building software, but it raises the value of engineering judgment, code review, and operational ownership.
expertLensFull.whatHappened: Bloomberg Technology reported on the spread of vibe coding, where non-programmers and developers use generative AI to turn plain-language prompts into working software.
expertLensFull.whyThisMatters: The shift makes small tools easier to start, but it does not remove the need for people who can test, secure, maintain, and explain the systems those tools become part of.
expertLensFull.marketMissing: The overlooked risk is mistaking a working prototype for production-ready software. Speed helps only if teams keep ownership, review, and accountability intact.
expertLensFull.investors: For investors, the useful question is whether companies can turn faster prototyping into durable products without creating hidden maintenance, security, or compliance debt.
expertLensFull.operators: For operators, AI coding tools may speed internal workflow fixes, but they do not remove the need for testing and clear accountability when software touches real infrastructure.
expertLensFull.hyperscalers: For hyperscalers and cloud providers, the opportunity is demand for stronger developer platforms, managed guardrails, and compute that supports heavier AI-assisted engineering workflows.
expertLensFull.watchNext: Watch hiring patterns for junior developers, enterprise controls around AI-generated code, and whether teams can measure productivity gains after the novelty wears off.
headlineOptions: Vibe coding is spreading, but engineering is not going away
AI can draft the code. Someone still has to own it
Why vibe coding raises the bar for software teams
The real test for AI coding is what happens after the demo
Vibe coding shifts the work, but not the responsibility
expertLensFull.finalHeadline: Vibe coding is spreading, but engineering is not going away
expertLensFull.metaDescription: Generative AI is making software easier to start, but businesses still need engineers to review, secure, and maintain what AI helps create.

A warehouse owner can automate shipping work. A designer can try building an app. Professional developers are using the same tools to move faster.

That does not make software engineering disappear. It changes where the hard work sits. The first draft of an app may arrive faster, but someone still has to understand the system, test the edge cases, secure the data, and decide whether the code is safe enough to run inside a business.

The risk for companies is treating a working demo as a finished product. If teams skip review because the prototype feels easy, they can trade short-term speed for long-term maintenance debt. That matters as junior developer hiring slows and more organizations lean on AI tools before they have clear rules for ownership.

For AI infrastructure companies, vibe coding is also a demand signal. More people building with AI means more pressure on developer platforms, managed guardrails, cloud services, and compute. The winners will not be the teams that generate the most code. They will be the teams that turn AI-assisted coding into reliable software.

Watch whether enterprises can measure productivity gains after the novelty fades, and whether they pair faster code generation with stronger review, testing, and security practices.

expertLens: Vibe coding lowers the barrier to building software, but it raises the value of engineering judgment, code review, and operational ownership.

title: Inside the Booming Market for Dinosaur Fossils | Odd Lots
snippet: Two years ago, Citadel's Ken Griffin paid almost $45 million for a stegosaurus skeleton, making it the most expensive fossil ever sold at auction. So why are dinosaur bones joining the collections of millionaires instea…
contentText: Two years ago, Citadel's Ken Griffin paid almost $45 million for a stegosaurus skeleton, making it the most expensive fossil ever sold at auction. So why are dinosaur bones joining the collections of millionaires instead of museums? How does the private market for fossils actually work? And how similar is it to the market for art and other antiquities? In this episode, we speak with Salomon Aaron, a director at London-based gallery David Aaron, where he is the gallery's in-house broker for dinosaur fossils. We talk about how fossils are found and priced, what it's like to work alongside dinosaur hunters, how his gallery identifies potential buyers, and why Joe thinks something about the birds-to-dinosaurs evolutionary pipeline is off. (Source: Bloomberg)

snippet: Bloomberg’s Caroline Hyde and Ed Ludlow discuss Apple’s strong outlook amid the continued memory supply crunch. Plus, Roblox CEO Dave Baszucki discusses the gaming platform’s "aggressive push to enhance safety" that tem…
contentText: Bloomberg’s Caroline Hyde and Ed Ludlow discuss Apple’s strong outlook amid the continued memory supply crunch. Plus, Roblox CEO Dave Baszucki discusses the gaming platform’s "aggressive push to enhance safety" that tempered user growth. And, Bloomberg spoke with OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar who pushes back on a report that the AI leader is missing its own targets. (Source: Bloomberg)
